evals.prompt: Initialize a brownfield project with Repo Church. I want the moat defined by default, state tracked deterministically, and the result ready to integrate into the Repo Bible.
evals.expected_output: Uses the church init/moat workflow, creates or references state and moat artifacts, produces elevator-pitch and third-party moat summaries, and identifies Bible integration tasks.

evals.prompt: We have a repo-bible packet and a rough roadmap. Run Repo Church intake and identify what must be hardened before planning the next implementation phase.
evals.expected_output: Uses or explicitly skips CLI inventory/HTML intake, consumes Bible artifacts, emits intake anchors, produces a thin roadmap slice, and routes unverified assumptions to the hardening gate.

evals.prompt: Review this phase plan against the Repo Church lifecycle. I want stricter acceptance criteria, research-backed assumptions, and a clear handoff for execution.
evals.expected_output: Produces anchor/spec gate findings, assumption confidence labels, closure tasks, validation/source/claim-scan usage notes, and an execution handoff with validation commands.

evals.prompt: The phase implementation is done. Run collaborative UAT and decide whether we can progress to the next phase or need mutual signoff.
evals.expected_output: Builds a UAT matrix from requirements, uses rendered HTML review when useful, records objective checks, identifies failures, and states whether agent/user mutual signoff is required.

evals.prompt: Our GSD-style workflow is getting too slow and repetitive. Optimize the lifecycle while preserving the gates that prevent bad execution plans.
evals.expected_output: Maps redundant steps, replaces repeated broad reads with CLI/HTML workbench substitutions, proposes safe parallel streams, keeps reliability guardrails, and avoids removing high-value gates just to save time.
